paragraphs.body: In the article, “Can Music improve our health and quality of life?” Lorrie Kubicek endorses the idea that Music boosts our mood and well-being and may even be a potential treatment option in a therapeutic setting. Kubicek begins by affirming this idea using evidence from a recent systematic review and meta-analysis (a study of studies) showing that the use of music intervention (listening to music, singing, and music therapy) can create significant improvements in physical health and even overall quality of life. The researchers also found no specific type of music or intervention that works universally. Kubicek goes on to note how complex our relationship is with music as human beings. She compares our relationship with music to a complicated dance that, “Shifts from moment to moment based on our mood, preferences, social situation and previous experiences.” Kubicek lists some methods of music therapy such as singing, instrument exploration, songwriting, music conversation, and reminiscence of music. She claims that music can be used to decrease anxiety and pain perception while increasing motivation and personal expression. Kubicek expands on her own top music therapy tools such as listening, playing an instrument, and singing. Finally, the Kubicek argues that music can be a powerful agent of change and can make hard things a little easier in life. It is ok to have hobbies, but you need to grow up eventually and become a doctor. My father has never said this to me directly, but it is implied. He would support me if I wanted to be something else other than a doctor, but it would be incredibly difficult for him to accept it. My father has been an attending surgeon for over twenty years. How can I blame him for this, it’s not his fault he grew with much more pressure than I have ever had to deal with. As a young boy in Iran, my grandfather sat my father and uncle down and said, “You will become a doctor, and you will become an engineer.” That tone was set for the rest of his life at only ten years old. He and his family started from nothing when they emigrated to the U.S. four decades ago, so it was not an easy road for them. He has a hard time understanding that it is ok to get a college degree in an art like music because it is simply so foreign to him. Pursuing an art was never an option, it just doesn’t make sense from his perspective because it is not safe in terms of seeing some return on your investment into the music or art or sculpting or whatever it may be. It is frustrating to me because I want to continue to pursue medicine and music. My mother grew up in the Pennsylvania and even went to the same high school I did. She has been a Physician Assistant specialized in pediatrics all her life and she loved that job. She was also my biggest musical influence growing up. Artists such as Dave Matthews and Bob Dylan have a big influence on my music. My parents are divorced and on long car-rides out to my mother’s home next the river I captured vivid memories of Gravedigger and Two-step among other great tunes playing in the background. She encourages my siblings and I to do something we are passionate about. She says if you find something you love, go after it and trust in yourself that everything will work out in the end. She supports music and medicine. Kubicek wrote, “Music can be a powerful agent of change.” My mother taught me this throughout my life by using song and dance to help me learn to express myself growing up.

paragraphs.body: I had never gone to a music lesson in my life and when I took the required course in 6th grade, I was largely uninterested. In fact, I really didn’t care about listening to music or playing it until I reached high school. It simply wasn’t something I thought about, I was more concerned with running and playing and being outside. I grew up in a ‘music-less world’ so to speak. I picked up the mandolin last winter and have learned very quickly that I am a quick learner when it comes to picking up instruments. People are amazed I learned so quickly, I already took the college course offered by Pitt for the mandolin and it was truly wonderful. In college I picked up the guitar. Now my friends cannot believe that I have only been playing for four or five months. I am very happy that I started these musical pursuits, but I am also saddened. Where was this feeling all my life? Why now? Why did I not take the time to check music out? Well, I can’t go back in time so it is here and now where I decide what I will do with my life, and I choose to continue to pursue medicine and my interest in music. I do feel torn between the expectations of my father and pursuing music. Duality exists within myself; my parents are divorced and have different ideals and expectations. I hope my dad understands that I can do both medicine and music. As Kubicek pointed out, “music interventions can create significant improvements in mental health, and even small improvements in physical health.” Look Dad, music and medicine can mix. I can help people and become a doctor and even help people with music. I am both music and medicine, like my mother and father are both my parents. They coexist, weave together, and intertwine and I am not complete unless both of them stay in my life, at least until I graduate from college.

annotations.text: already took the college course offered by Pitt for the mandolin and it was truly wonderful.
Duality exists within myself; my parents are divorced and have different ideals and expectations.
annotations.comment: Aha. Yes. Friction and heat. This feels like an important big idea to develop however. As of now, your essay's entire "so what" and center of gravity is summarized (rather than developed) in these last few sentences. My sense is that this "duality" is important, but your essay needs to show it to your reader. Show us how and why you have competing interests and goals as they relate to your parents.
